entities.name: chimeric antigen receptor T regulatory cells
Department of Clinical Science, University of Bergen
Department of Medicine, Haukeland University Hospital
Autoimmune primary adrenal insufficiency -current diagnostic approaches and future perspectives

evidence_entities.text: The adrenal glands are small endocrine glands located on top of each kidney, producing hormones regulating important functions in our body like metabolism and stress.
There are several underlying causes for adrenal insufficiency, where an autoimmune attack by the immune system is the most common cause.

The adrenal cortex is responsible for producing glucocorticoids, mineralocorticoids, and adrenal androgens whereas the medulla produces adrenaline and noradrenaline, also referred to as catecholamines.
Primary AI (PAI), also known as Addison's disease, is a rare disease with a prevalence of approximately 100 per million in Europe.
The most common cause of primary adrenal insufficiency is autoimmunity; this type is often called Autoimmune Addison's Disease, or simply autoimmune PAI.
Morning cortisol concentration <140 nmol/L and an ACTH concentration twice the upper reference limit verifies a PAI diagnosis.

A number of genes are known to confer early onset adrenal disease in monogenic inheritance patterns, including genetic defects in enzymes of adrenal steroidogenesis (e.g., CYP21A1 and CYP11A1), the ACTH receptor (MC2R) and molecules involved in establishing the mitochondrial redox potential like nicotinamide nucleotide transhydrogenase (NNT) and thioredoxin reductase 2 (TRXR2) amongst others.
The autoantibodies targeting 21-OH was first described in 1992 by Winqvist et al., identified as a major autoantigen in autoimmune PAI.
Two immunodominant peptides have been found to be recognized by CD8+ T cells.

properties.note: adrenaline and noradrenaline are referred to as catecholamines
adrenaline and noradrenaline are referred to as catecholamines
autoimmune PAI is the most common cause of primary adrenal insufficiency

notes: This review paper covers autoimmune primary adrenal insufficiency, its diagnosis, genetics, and treatment approaches
Multiple gene variants and autoantibodies are discussed as risk factors and diagnostic markers
The paper emphasizes the polygenic nature of autoimmune PAI and potential for early identification
